selected_base.name: Shiodome / Shimbashi
selected_base.transit_summary: Central, covered-walkway base with direct Narita access via the Yamanote/JR Shimbashi hub and Toei Oedo/Asakusa lines; walkable to Ginza and Hamarikyu, and an easy hop to Roppongi (Grand Hyatt) and Akasaka (Harry Potter Cafe). Underground concourses make rainy arrival days painless.
selected_base.rationale: Best fit for this Tokyo place list: Shiodome sits between Ginza and the bay, gives a one-seat-ish ride to Narita for the Scoot arrival, and keeps the saved Roppongi/Akasaka/Ariake stops within a short ride. Dense convenience-store and dining access supports late-night ramen and walking-friendly evenings.
tradeoffs: Business-district quiet at night versus livelier Shinjuku or Shibuya.
Tokyo Dream Park in Ariake still needs a Yurikamome/transit leg.
Central rates run above outer-ward value hotels.

base_areas.name: Shiodome / Shimbashi
base_areas.transit_summary: Central, covered-walkway base with direct Narita access via the Shimbashi hub; walkable to Ginza, easy to Roppongi and the Ariake bayfront.
base_areas.rationale: Strongest all-around base for the Tokyo cluster: airport access, Ginza walkability, and short rides to the saved Roppongi/Akasaka/Ariake spots.
tradeoffs: Ariake still needs a transit leg.

base_areas.name: Ginza
base_areas.transit_summary: Premium shopping/dining district one stop from Shimbashi, with broad subway coverage toward Akasaka and Roppongi.
base_areas.rationale: A polished, very walkable alternative immediately north of Shiodome that keeps the same airport and cross-city access while adding flagship dining.
tradeoffs: Less late-night casual food than Shimbashi.

base_areas.name: Shinjuku
base_areas.transit_summary: Major interchange with huge dining/nightlife density and strong rail, but farther from the bayfront Ariake stop.
base_areas.rationale: Great value-and-energy base if nightlife matters more than proximity to the saved eastern/bayfront places.
tradeoffs: Longer rides to Ariake and Shiodome.
Busier and more crowded at peak times.

base_areas.name: Roppongi
base_areas.transit_summary: Close to Grand Hyatt and Akasaka, with Hibiya/Oedo line access, but a longer reach to the bay and Narita.
base_areas.rationale: Convenient if the trip centers on the Roppongi/Akasaka saved spots, at the cost of a longer airport and Ariake connection.
tradeoffs: Less direct Narita access than Shiodome.

hotel_candidates.name: The Royal Park Hotel Iconic Tokyo Shiodome
hotel_candidates.price_summary: Mid-range to upper-mid. Direct-access Shiodome tower with strong rail links to Narita and walkable Ginza/Shimbashi.
hotel_candidates.rationale: Best pick: meets every requirement — central Shiodome base with covered station access (great for the rainy arrival days), walking-friendly to Ginza/Shimbashi dining, and an easy NRT transfer that fits the Scoot arrival.
tradeoffs: Not the cheapest option in the shortlist.
Shiodome is business-district quiet at night versus livelier Shinjuku/Shibuya.

hotel_candidates.name: Park Hotel Tokyo (Shiodome)
hotel_candidates.price_summary: Mid-range. Art-themed rooms in the Shiodome Media Tower, same walkable cluster.
hotel_candidates.rationale: Strong runner-up in the same base: similar Shiodome convenience and Ginza walkability at a slightly more design-forward, sometimes lower price point.
tradeoffs: Rooms can be smaller than the best pick.
Higher floors fill fast on popular dates.

hotel_candidates.name: Conrad Tokyo (Shiodome)
hotel_candidates.price_summary: Luxury. Bayfront Shiodome tower with spa and skyline views.
hotel_candidates.rationale: Comfort/upgrade option in the same base for travelers willing to spend more: larger rooms, spa, and premium dining while keeping the identical Shiodome transit advantages.
tradeoffs: Well above the mid-range budget for this trip.
Premium amenities are nice-to-have, not required by the stated preferences.
